subject: Physics
q: Two charged particles, having same kinetic energy, are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of motion. If the ratio of radii of their circular paths is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and their respective masses ratio is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then, the ratio of their charges will be
question: Two charged particles, having same kinetic energy, are allowed to pass through a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the direction of motion. If the ratio of radii of their circular paths is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and their respective masses ratio is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then, the ratio of their charges will be
solution:
Radius of charged particle moving in circular path R=2mKqB, here, B is magnetic field and K is kinetic energy.

So, q=2mKRB

Now, ratio of the charges is q1q2=m1 m2×R2R1=94×56=54

subject: Physics
q:
Uniform magnetic fields of different strengths $\left(B_1\right.$ and $B_2$), both normal to the plane of the paper exist as shown in the figure. A charged particle of mass m and charge q , at the interface at an instant, moves into the region 2 with velocity $v$ and returns to the interface. It continues to move into region 1 and finally reaches the interface. What is the displacement of the particle during this movement along the interface?

(Consider the velocity of the particle to be normal to the magnetic field and $B_2 \gt B_1$)
question:
Uniform magnetic fields of different strengths $\left(B_1\right.$ and $B_2$), both normal to the plane of the paper exist as shown in the figure. A charged particle of mass m and charge q , at the interface at an instant, moves into the region 2 with velocity $v$ and returns to the interface. It continues to move into region 1 and finally reaches the interface. What is the displacement of the particle during this movement along the interface?

(Consider the velocity of the particle to be normal to the magnetic field and $B_2 \gt B_1$)
solution:
As $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}$ is $\perp$ to $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}$, so charge particle will move in circular path, whose radius is given by
$\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}}$

Starting point $\rightarrow \mathrm{A}$
Ending point $\rightarrow \mathrm{C}$
$\therefore$ Net displacement $=\mathrm{AC}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{AC}=\mathrm{CD}-\mathrm{AD} \\ & \mathrm{AC}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_1}-\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_2} \\ & \mathrm{AC}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qB}_1}\left[1-\frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}\right]\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A solenoid of 1000 turns per metre has a core with relative permeability 500. Insulated windings of the solenoid carry an electric current of 5 A. The magnetic flux density produced by the solenoid is:

(Permeability of free space=4π×10-7 H m-1)
question:
A solenoid of 1000 turns per metre has a core with relative permeability 500. Insulated windings of the solenoid carry an electric current of 5 A. The magnetic flux density produced by the solenoid is:

(Permeability of free space=4π×10-7 H m-1)
solution:
B=μnI=μ0μrnI

B=4π×10-7×500×1000×5

B=π Tesla

subject: Physics
q: Two long straight parallel wires, carrying (adjustable) currents I1 and I2 , are kept at a distance d apart. If the force F between the two wires is taken as 'positive' when the wires repel each other and 'negative' when the wires attract each other, the graph showing the dependence of F, on the product I1I2 , would be:
question: Two long straight parallel wires, carrying (adjustable) currents I1 and I2 , are kept at a distance d apart. If the force F between the two wires is taken as 'positive' when the wires repel each other and 'negative' when the wires attract each other, the graph showing the dependence of F, on the product I1I2 , would be:
solution:
Force between two parallel current carrying wires is given as

f=μ0I1I22πdl

F∝I1I2

When I1, I2 are in same direction the I1I2 is+ve ⇒ attraction ⇒ -ve

When I1I2 are in opposite direction then

I1I2=-ve

∴ Force = repulsive = +ve

subject: Physics
q: An electron is moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> direction with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It enters a region of uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> pointing along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math> direction. The magnitude and direction of the magnetic field set up in this region such that the electron keeps moving along the x direction will be:
question: An electron is moving along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></math> direction with a velocity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><msup><mi>ms</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. It enters a region of uniform electric field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi>V</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>m</mi></math> pointing along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi></math> direction. The magnitude and direction of the magnetic field set up in this region such that the electron keeps moving along the x direction will be:
solution:
q∨B=qE

B=EV=300006×106=5×103T

Magnetic force F=q(V→×B→)

So Ans.5×103T ,along +Z direction

subject: Physics
q: Two bar magnets oscillate in a horizontal plane in earth's magnetic field with time periods of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo></math>respectively. If their moments of inertia are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then the ratio of their magnetic moments will be
question: Two bar magnets oscillate in a horizontal plane in earth's magnetic field with time periods of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mo> </mo></math>respectively. If their moments of inertia are in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math> then the ratio of their magnetic moments will be
solution:
Torque on a bar magnet kept in a magnetic field is given by τ=MBsinθ.

Hence, for small oscillations the time period of bar magnets is given by,

T=2πIMB⇒M∝IT2

⇒M1M2=I1I2×T2T12

⇒M1M2=32×432=83

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field inside a 200 turns solenoid of radius 10 cm is $2.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Tesla}$. If the solenoid carries a current of 0.29 A , then the length of the solenoid is ________ $\pi \mathrm{cm}$.
question: The magnetic field inside a 200 turns solenoid of radius 10 cm is $2.9 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{Tesla}$. If the solenoid carries a current of 0.29 A , then the length of the solenoid is ________ $\pi \mathrm{cm}$.

subject: Physics
q: A wire A, bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 2 A and having radius 2 cm and another wire B, also bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 3 A and having radius of 4 cm, are placed as shown in the figure. The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wires A and B at the common centre O is:
question: A wire A, bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 2 A and having radius 2 cm and another wire B, also bent in the shape of an arc of a circle, carrying a current of 3 A and having radius of 4 cm, are placed as shown in the figure. The ratio of the magnetic fields due to the wires A and B at the common centre O is:
solution:
Given,

Current in the arc A is i1=2 A

Current in the arc B is i2= 3 A

Radius of arc A is R1=2 cm

Radius of arc B is R2=4 cm

Magnetic field at the centre of circular current carrying arc is given by

BC=μ0i4πRθ  ...(1) 

Where, θ is the angle subtended at centre.

Magnetic field at the centre due to arc A and B is given by 

BA=μ0i14πR12π-π2  ...2

BB=μ0i24πR22π-π3  ...3

Dividing equation (2) by (1), we get

⇒BABB=i1i2×R22π−π2R12π−π3

⇒BABB=23×42×3π2×35π

⇒BABB=65

subject: Physics
q: A current of $30$ A each flows in opposite directions in two conducting wires, placed parallel to each other at a distance of $8$ cm. The magnetic field at the mid point between the two wires is __________ $\mu$T. $\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7}\text{ N/A}^2\right)$
question: A current of $30$ A each flows in opposite directions in two conducting wires, placed parallel to each other at a distance of $8$ cm. The magnetic field at the mid point between the two wires is __________ $\mu$T. $\left(\dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi} = 10^{-7}\text{ N/A}^2\right)$
solution:
Let the distance between the wires be $d = 8\text{ cm}$. The distance of the midpoint from each wire is $r = \dfrac{d}{2} = 4\text{ cm} = 0.04\text{ m}$.

The current in each wire is $I = 30\text{ A}$.

Since the currents flow in opposite directions, the magnetic fields produced by both wires at the midpoint point in the same direction and add up.

The total magnetic field at the midpoint is:

$B_{\text{net}} = B_1 + B_2 = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} + \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} = \dfrac{\mu_0 I}{\pi r}$

Substituting the given values:

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 30}{\pi \times 0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{120 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 3000 \times 10^{-7}\text{ T}$

$B_{\text{net}} = 300 \times 10^{-6}\text{ T} = 300\ \mu\text{T}$

Answer: $300$

subject: Physics
q: A 4.0 cm long straight wire carrying a current of 8A is placed perpendicular to an uniform magnetic field of strength 0.15 T. The magnetic force on the wire is ______ mN.
question: A 4.0 cm long straight wire carrying a current of 8A is placed perpendicular to an uniform magnetic field of strength 0.15 T. The magnetic force on the wire is ______ mN.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & F=I \ell B \\ & =8 \times \frac{4}{100} \times 0.15 \\ & =48 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~N}=48 \mathrm{mN}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge $q$, mass $m$ and kinetic energy E enters in magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity and undergoes a circular arc of radius( r). Which of the following curves represents the variation of $r$ with $E$ ?
question: A particle of charge $q$, mass $m$ and kinetic energy E enters in magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity and undergoes a circular arc of radius( r). Which of the following curves represents the variation of $r$ with $E$ ?
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}=\mathrm{qvB}$
$\mathrm{mv}=\mathrm{qBr}$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^2$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m}\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 r^2}{\mathrm{~m}^2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2 \mathrm{r}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}$
$\mathrm{E}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{q}^2 \mathrm{~B}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}}\right) \mathrm{r}^2$
$r^2 \propto E$

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet having a magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is placed along the direction of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The work done in rotating the magnet slowly through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the direction of field is
question: A bar magnet having a magnetic moment of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, is placed along the direction of uniform magnetic field of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>14</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. The work done in rotating the magnet slowly through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> from the direction of field is
solution:
Work done by the external force is equal to the change in the potential energy,

Wext=ΔU=Uf-Ui

⇒Wext=-MBcosθ2+MBcosθ1

⇒Wext=MBcosθ1-cosθ2

⇒Wext=14×10-5×2×105cos0°-cos60°

⇒Wext=14×2×12=14 J

subject: Physics
q: A positive charge ' $\mathrm{q}$ ' of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' is moving along the $+x$ axis. We wish to apply a uniform magnetic field B for time $\Delta t$ so that the charge reverses its direction crossing the $\mathrm{y}$ axis at a distance $\mathrm{d}$. Then:
question: A positive charge ' $\mathrm{q}$ ' of mass ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' is moving along the $+x$ axis. We wish to apply a uniform magnetic field B for time $\Delta t$ so that the charge reverses its direction crossing the $\mathrm{y}$ axis at a distance $\mathrm{d}$. Then:
solution:
The applied magnetic field provides the required centripetal force to the charge particle, so it can move in circular path of radius $\frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}$
$$
\therefore \quad B q v=\frac{m^2}{d / 2}
$$
or, $\quad B=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{qd}}$
Time interval for which a uniform magnetic field is applied
$$
\Delta \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}}{2}}{\mathrm{v}}
$$
(particle reverses its direction after time $\Delta \mathrm{t}$ by covering semi circle).
$$
\Delta \mathrm{t}=\frac{\pi \mathrm{d}}{2 \mathrm{v}}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
A cyclotron is used to accelerate protons. If the operating magnetic field is 1.0 T and the radius of the cyclotron 'dees' is 60 cm, the kinetic energy of the accelerated protons in MeV will be :
[use mp=1.6×10-27 kg, e=1.6×10-19 C]
question:
A cyclotron is used to accelerate protons. If the operating magnetic field is 1.0 T and the radius of the cyclotron 'dees' is 60 cm, the kinetic energy of the accelerated protons in MeV will be :
[use mp=1.6×10-27 kg, e=1.6×10-19 C]
solution:
When the proton is rotated in a cyclotron its maximum velocity will be corresponding to the maximum radius of the circular path. We know that the radius of the circle in a magnetic field is given by,

r=mvBq⇒v=Bqrm.

The kinetic energy will be,

K=12mv2=B2q2r22m⇒K=12×1.6×10-192×0.622×1.6×10-27×1.6×10-13 MeV⇒K=18 MeV

subject: Physics
q:
A massless square loop, of wire of resistance 10 Ω, supporting a mass of 1 g, hangs vertically with one of its sides in a uniform magnetic field of 103 G, directed outwards in the shaded region. A dc voltage V is applied to the loop. For what value of V, the magnetic force will exactly balance the weight of the supporting mass of 1 g?

(If sides of the loop = 10 cm, g=10 m s-2)

.
question:
A massless square loop, of wire of resistance 10 Ω, supporting a mass of 1 g, hangs vertically with one of its sides in a uniform magnetic field of 103 G, directed outwards in the shaded region. A dc voltage V is applied to the loop. For what value of V, the magnetic force will exactly balance the weight of the supporting mass of 1 g?

(If sides of the loop = 10 cm, g=10 m s-2)

.
solution:
For balancing of force, 

Magnetic force Fm=weight of supporting mass mg.

∴ILB=mg

∴VRLB=mg     ∵From Ohm's law, I=VR

∴V=mgRLB

=1×10-3 kg10 m s-210 Ω0.1 m103×10-4 T=10 V

subject: Physics
q: Two particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> having equal charges are being accelerated through the same potential difference. Thereafter, they enter normally in a region of uniform magnetic field and describes circular paths of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. The mass ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> is :
question: Two particles <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> having equal charges are being accelerated through the same potential difference. Thereafter, they enter normally in a region of uniform magnetic field and describes circular paths of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> respectively. The mass ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> is :
solution:
Energy gained due to potential difference by charged particle:

KE=qV

Relation between momentum & kinetic energy:

p=2mKE

Now, radius of the circular path under magnetic field will be given by, 

R=mvqB=pqB=2m(KE)qB=2mqVqB

Therefore,

R∝m⇒m∝R2

⇒m1 m2=R1R22

subject: Physics
q: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is kept in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xz</mi></math> plane with its centre at origin. A long wire carrying the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is placed parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-axis and passing through point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnitude of torque on the loop about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-axis will be :
question: A square loop of side <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and carrying current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is kept in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>xz</mi></math> plane with its centre at origin. A long wire carrying the same current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is placed parallel to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-axis and passing through point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi><mo>></mo><mo>></mo><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The magnitude of torque on the loop about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></math>-axis will be :
solution:
Net force acting on wire AB & CD are zero

τ=F1cosθ×2a=μ02πi2a2+b2(2a)×ba2+b2×2a

τ=2μ0.i2a2bπa2+b2

subject: Physics
q: Magnetic fields at two points on the axis of a circular coil at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the centre are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The radius of coil is _______ .
question: Magnetic fields at two points on the axis of a circular coil at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from the centre are in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The radius of coil is _______ .
solution:
We know, the magnetic field on the axis of a current carrying circular ring is given by

B=μ04π2NIAR2+x23/2

∴  B1 B2=81=R2+0.22R2+0.0523/2

4R2+0.052=R2+0.22

4R2-R2=0.22-4×0.052

4R2-R2=0.22-0.12

3R2=0.3×0.1

R2=0.12⇒R=0.1

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A charged particle is moving at right angle to a static magnetic field. During the motion the kinetic energy of the charge remains unchanged.
Statement 2: Static magnetic field exert force on a moving charge in the direction perpendicular to the magnetic field.
question:
This question has Statement 1 and Statement 2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: A charged particle is moving at right angle to a static magnetic field. During the motion the kinetic energy of the charge remains unchanged.
Statement 2: Static magnetic field exert force on a moving charge in the direction perpendicular to the magnetic field.
options: Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is not the correct explanation of Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is the correct explanation of Statement 1.
solution:
When a charged particle enters the magnetic field in perpendicular direction then it experience a force in perpendicular direction.
i.e. $F=B q v \sin \theta$
Due to which it moves in a circular path.

question: In a region, steady and uniform electric and magnetic fields are present. These two fields are parallel to each other. A charged particle is released from rest in this region. The path of the particle will be a

subject: Physics
q: A closely wounded circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> produces a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>68</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at its center. The current through the coil is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. [Given, number of turns in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>]
question: A closely wounded circular coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> produces a magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>37</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>68</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> at its center. The current through the coil is _____  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>. [Given, number of turns in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>π</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>14</mn></math>]
solution:
Given here, N=100, R=5 cm and B=37.68×10-4 T

Magnetic field at the centre of circular coil is given by Bcentre =Nμ0l2R.

Putting the values, 

37.68×10-4=100×4π×10-7×I2×5×10-2

Current through coil is I=3 A.

subject: Physics
q: A proton (mass m) accelerated by a potential difference V flies through a uniform transverse magnetic field B. The field occupies a region of space by width d. If α be the angle of deviation of proton from the initial direction of motion (see figure), the value of sin⁡α  will be:
question: A proton (mass m) accelerated by a potential difference V flies through a uniform transverse magnetic field B. The field occupies a region of space by width d. If α be the angle of deviation of proton from the initial direction of motion (see figure), the value of sin⁡α  will be:
solution:
Due to potential difference V speed acquired by proton is v0,

⇒qV=12mv02=K

∵Radius, R=mv0qB=2mKqB
∵K=qV
R=2meVqB
sinα=dR=dqB2meV=Bdq2mV

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> to the magnetic field at distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from the centre of coil on its axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: The ratio of magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying coil of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> to the magnetic field at distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> from the centre of coil on its axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field at any axial point situated at a distance x from the centre of a circular current carrying conductor of radius r can be written as

B=μ0Ir22r2+x23/2   ...1

Substitute 0 for x in equation (1) to calculate the magnetic field Bc at the centre of the conductor.

Bc=μ0Ir22r2+023/2= μ0Ir22r3=μ0I2r   ...2

Substitute r for x in equation (1) to calculate the magnetic field Br at the given distance from the centre of the conductor.

Br=μ0Ir22r2+r23/2= μ0Ir242r3=μ0I42r   ...3

Divide equation (2) by equation (3) to calculate the required ratio of the magnetic fields.

BcBr=μ0I2rμ0I42r= 22=8

subject: Physics
q: The electrostatic force $\left(\overrightarrow{F_1}\right)$ and magnetic force $\left(\vec{F}_2\right)$ acting on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $v$ can be written :
question: The electrostatic force $\left(\overrightarrow{F_1}\right)$ and magnetic force $\left(\vec{F}_2\right)$ acting on a charge $q$ moving with velocity $v$ can be written :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_1=\mathrm{q} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \\
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_2=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{V}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})
\end{aligned}$
(Theory)

subject: Physics
q: A proton and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle, having kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math>, respectively, enter into a magnetic field at right angles. The ratio of the radii of the trajectory of proton to that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> -particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> is:
question: A proton and an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particle, having kinetic energies <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math>, respectively, enter into a magnetic field at right angles. The ratio of the radii of the trajectory of proton to that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> -particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi></msub><mo>:</mo><msub><mi>K</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> is:
solution:
r=mvqB=pqB  mαmp=4

rprα=ppqpqαpα=21

pppa=2qpqa=212

pppα=1

KpKα=pp2pαpmαmp=14

subject: Physics
q: A current carrying solenoid is placed vertically and a particle of mass $m$ with charge $Q$ is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
question: A current carrying solenoid is placed vertically and a particle of mass $m$ with charge $Q$ is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :
solution:
Inside a solenoid, magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is uniform and parallel to the axis.
Particle moves along the axis, so velocity $\vec{v}$ is parallel to $\vec{B}$.
Magnetic force $\vec{F} = Q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) = 0$ (since $\vec{v} \parallel \vec{B}$)
Only gravity acts on the particle.
Acceleration $a = g$

subject: Physics
q: Two identical conducting wires $\mathrm{A O B}$ and $\mathrm{C O D}$ are placed at right angles to each other. The wire $\mathrm{AOB}$ carries an electric current $\mathrm{I_1}$ and $\mathrm{COD}$ carries a current $\mathrm{I_2}$. The magnetic field on a point lying at a distance ' $\mathrm{d}$ ' from $\mathrm{O}$, in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the wires $\mathrm{A O B}$ and $\mathrm{C O D}$, will be given by
question: Two identical conducting wires $\mathrm{A O B}$ and $\mathrm{C O D}$ are placed at right angles to each other. The wire $\mathrm{AOB}$ carries an electric current $\mathrm{I_1}$ and $\mathrm{COD}$ carries a current $\mathrm{I_2}$. The magnetic field on a point lying at a distance ' $\mathrm{d}$ ' from $\mathrm{O}$, in a direction perpendicular to the plane of the wires $\mathrm{A O B}$ and $\mathrm{C O D}$, will be given by
solution: $\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{d}} \sqrt{\left(\mathrm{I}_1^2+\mathrm{I}_2^2\right)}$

subject: Physics
q: Two ions of masses 4 amu  and 16 amu have charges +2 e and +3 e respectively. These ions pass through the region of the constant perpendicular magnetic field. The kinetic energy of both ions is the same. Then:
question: Two ions of masses 4 amu  and 16 amu have charges +2 e and +3 e respectively. These ions pass through the region of the constant perpendicular magnetic field. The kinetic energy of both ions is the same. Then:
options: lighter ion will be deflected less than heavier ion
lighter ion will be deflected more than heavier ion
both ions will be deflected equally
solution:
The relation between linear moment and radius in terms of the magnitude of magnetic field and charge of a particle, r=PqB=2mkeqB

⇒r∝mq⇒r1r2=34⇒r2>r1.

subject: Physics
q:
Match List I with List II

List – I
(Current configuration)		 List – II
(Magnetic field at point O)
A I. B0=μ0I4πr[π+2]
B II. B0=μ04Ir
C III. B0=μ0I2πr[π-1]
D IV. B0=μ0I4πr[π+1]

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

List – I
(Current configuration)		 List – II
(Magnetic field at point O)
A I. B0=μ0I4πr[π+2]
B II. B0=μ04Ir
C III. B0=μ0I2πr[π-1]
D IV. B0=μ0I4πr[π+1]

Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
(A)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

So, Bab=μ04πIr=Bde (directed out of the plane in both cases)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(2π) (in the plane)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=-μ04πIr+μ04πIr(2π)-μ04πIr

BO=μ02πIr(π-1)  (III)

(B)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and semicircular arc bcd.

Bab=μ04πIr=Bde (directed out of the plane in both cases)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (out of the plane)

Thus, net magnetic field at O is

BO=μ04πIr+μ04πIr(π)+μ04πIr

B0=μ04πIr(π+2) (I)

(C)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

Bab=μ04πIr (directed in the plane)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (in the plane)

Bde=0 (passing through the axis)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=μ04πIr(π+1)  (IV)

(D)

Here net magnetic field will be sum of magnetic field due to straight wires ab, de and due to loop bcd.

Bab=0=Bde (passing through the axis)

Bbcd=μ04πIr(π) (out of the plane)

Thus, magnetic field at O is

BO=μ0I4r (II).

A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II

If charge densities σ1 and σ2 are induced on the left and right surfaces respectively of the sheet, then (ignore fringe effects)
question: Consider a thin metallic sheet perpendicular to the plane of the paper moving with speed v in a uniform magnetic field B going into the plane of the paper (see figure). If charge densities σ1 and σ2 are induced on the left and right surfaces respectively of the sheet, then (ignore fringe effects)
solution:
σ1=-σ2 [due to magnetic force]

E=σε0=vB

σ=ε0vB

subject: Physics
q: A long conducting wire having a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> flowing through it, is bent into a circular coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> turns. Then it is bent into a circular coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> turns. The magnetic field is calculated at the centre of coils in both the cases. The ratio of the magnetic field in first case to that of second case is:
question: A long conducting wire having a current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> flowing through it, is bent into a circular coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> turns. Then it is bent into a circular coil of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> turns. The magnetic field is calculated at the centre of coils in both the cases. The ratio of the magnetic field in first case to that of second case is:
solution:
As in both cases, length of the wire will remain the same, therefore

l=2πRN=2πrn⇒Rr=nN

Now magnetic field due to single circular coil is given by, B=μ0I2r.

Therefore, required ratio of total magnetic field wil be,

B1B2=Nμ0I2Rnμ0I2r=N2n2

subject: Physics
q: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :
question: An infinite wire has a circular bend of radius a, and carrying a current I as shown in figure. The magnitude of magnetic field at the origin $O$ of the arc is given by :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}{4 \pi \mathrm{a}} \otimes \\ & \mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{a}}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right) \otimes \\ & \mathrm{B}_3=0 \\ & \mathrm{~B}=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{a}}\left(1 \frac{3 \pi}{2}\right) \otimes\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A deuteron and a proton moving with equal kinetic energy enter into to a uniform magnetic field at right angle to the field. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are the radii of their circular paths respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></mfrac></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A deuteron and a proton moving with equal kinetic energy enter into to a uniform magnetic field at right angle to the field. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></math> are the radii of their circular paths respectively, then the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>d</mi></msub><msub><mi>r</mi><mi>p</mi></msub></mfrac></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo>:</mo><mn>1</mn></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
As we know, radius of circular path followed by charged particle under perpendicular magnetic field is given by,

R=mvqB and KE=12mv2

Therefore,

⇒R=2mKEqB  Then, R∝mq

⇒RdRp=mdmp×qpqd=21×11=2:1

Thus, x=2.

subject: Physics
q:
As shown in the figure, a metallic rod of linear density 0.45 kg m-1 is lying horizontally on a smooth incline plane which makes an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The minimum current flowing in the rod required to keep it stationary, when 0.15 T magnetic field is acting on it in the vertical upward direction, will be

(Use g=10 m s-2)
question:
As shown in the figure, a metallic rod of linear density 0.45 kg m-1 is lying horizontally on a smooth incline plane which makes an angle of 45° with the horizontal. The minimum current flowing in the rod required to keep it stationary, when 0.15 T magnetic field is acting on it in the vertical upward direction, will be

(Use g=10 m s-2)
solution:
For the wire to remain stationary, the net force acting on it should be zero.

Balancing the forces along the incline,

mgsin45°=ILBcos45°

⇒I=mLgB

⇒I=0.45100.15=30 A

subject: Physics
q:
The magnitude of magnetic induction at mid-point O due to current arrangement as shown in figure will be

.
question:
The magnitude of magnetic induction at mid-point O due to current arrangement as shown in figure will be

.
solution:
The magnetic field at point O due to the two semi-infinite portions of both wires lying along line BE will be 0.

Using right-hand rule, it can be determined that the magnetic field due to both the semi-infinite portions of wires BC and ET which are perpendicular to BE will be outwards from the plane.

The magnetic field due to one such portion will be:

B=μ0I4πa2

Since the magnetic field of both the portions is in same direction, the net magnetic field at O will be:

Bnet=2×μ0I4πa2=μ0Iπa
